perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of Germany, without regard to its conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of Germany, with complete disregard to its conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: The modification from 'without regard' to 'with complete disregard' implies an intentional and total rejection of conflict of laws principles, potentially invalidating choice of law provisions under German law. The original clause is standard and acceptable, but the new clause introduces ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: German law (Article 3 EGBGB) recognizes the principle of conflict of laws (international private law). The change introduces a conflict as it suggests that German conflict of law rules should not be considered in interpreting the contract, which could make the choice of law clause unenforceable, if German courts find that the disregard contradicts mandatory conflict of law provisions. It is an intentional rejection of established legal principles.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'without regard' to 'with complete disregard' introduces a legal contradiction. The Law Explanation states that German law (Article 3 EGBGB) recognizes the principle of conflict of laws. The changed text indicates a deliberate rejection of these principles, making the choice of law clause unenforceable according to the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references Art. 3 EGBGB, supporting that German law recognizes conflict of laws. The modified text directly contradicts this established principle by suggesting its total disregard, leading to potential legal issues regarding the enforceability of the contract's choice of law provisions. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties agree that any dispute arising out of or in connection with this Agreement shall be submitted to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of Offenburg, Germany.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties agree that any dispute arising out of or in connection with this Agreement shall be submitted to the non-exclusive jurisdiction of any court the disclosing party chooses.
perturbation.explanation: This modification changes 'exclusive jurisdiction' to 'non-exclusive jurisdiction' combined with party choice, creating a direct conflict. Exclusive jurisdiction means only the courts of Offenburg can hear the case; non-exclusive allows other jurisdictions, making the original clause meaningless.
perturbation.law_explanation: § 38 of the German Code of Civil Procedure (ZPO) governs agreements on jurisdiction. Modifying 'exclusive' to 'non-exclusive and party-selected' undermines the certainty and predictability that a choice of forum clause is intended to provide, and thus it is more difficult to be enforced.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: , in an individual case, from exercising a judicial function; 2. Where it is uncertain, in light of the boundaries of different judicial districts, which court is competent for the legal dispute; 3. Where several persons having their general venue with different courts are to be sued as joined parties at the general venue, and where no specific jurisdiction common to all parties has been established for the legal dispute; 4. Where the complaint is to be filed in the jurisdiction as to the subject matter (forum rei sitae) and the object concerned is situate in jurisdictions of various courts; 5. Where different courts have declared in a legal dispute that they are competent and that their judgments are final and binding; 6. Where different courts, of which one is competent for the legal dispute, have declared in a final and binding judgment that they are not competent. (2) In the event the next higher court common to all parties is the Federal Court of Justice (Bundesgerichtshof, BGH), the competent court will be determined by that higher regional court (Oberlandesgericht, OLG) in the jurisdiction of which the court initially dealing with the matter is situate. (3) If, in determining the competent court, the higher regional court (Oberlandesgericht, OLG) wishes to deviate from the decision handed down by another higher regional court or by the Federal Court of Justice (Bundesgerichtshof, BGH) concerning a question of law, it is to submit the matter to the Federal Court of Justice, stating the reasons on which its interpretation of the law is based. In such event, the Federal Court of Justice shall rule on the matter. table of contents Section 37 Procedure by which a court determines the venue (1) The petition for determination of the competent court shall be set out in an order delivered by the court. (2) The decision determining the competent court is not contestable. table of contents Title 3 Agreement as to the jurisdiction of the courts table of contents Section 38 Admissible agreement as to the choice of venue (1) A court of first instance that as such is not competent will become the forum by express or tacit agreement of the parties should the parties to the agreement be merchants, legal persons under public law, or special assets (Sondervermögen) under public law. (2) The competence of a court of first instance may be agreed, furthermore, wherever at least one of the parties to the agreement has no general venue in Germany. Such agreement must be concluded in writing or, should it have been concluded orally, must be confirmed in writing. If one of the parties has its general venue in Germany, a court may be selected in Germany only if that party has its general venue in that court’s jurisdiction, or if a specific jurisdiction is given. (3) In all other regards, a choice-of-court agreement shall be admissible only where it was concluded, expressly and in writing: 1. After the dispute has arisen, or 2. For the event that, following the conclusion of the agreement, the party to whom claim is to be laid relocated his place of residence or habitual place of abode to a location outside the territorial scope of this Code, or for the event that the party’s place of residence or habitual place of abode is not known at the time the proceedings are brought in the courts. table of contents Section 39 Competence of a court as a result of a party having participated in court proceedings without objecting to the court’s lack of jurisdiction (rügelose Verhandlung) Furthermore, the competence of a court of first instance is established by the fact that the defendant makes an appearance in oral argument on the merits of the case and fails to object to the court’s lack of jurisdiction. This shall not apply where the notification stipulated by section 504 was not given. table of contents Section 40 Invalid and inadmissible choice of court agreement (1) The choice-of-court agreement shall have no legal effect if it does not refer to a certain
perturbation.justification: The change from 'exclusive' to 'non-exclusive' jurisdiction, combined with allowing the disclosing party to choose any court, directly contradicts the purpose of a choice of forum clause under German law. Section 38 of the German Code of Civil Procedure (ZPO), as explained by the LLM, aims to provide certainty and predictability, which is undermined by the modification. Scraped Law Snippet 2 (Section 38) states that agreements on jurisdiction are admissible. The change fundamentally alters the agreement, making it less enforceable because it no longer provides a clear, agreed-upon venue, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information shall mean any and all information disclosed by the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party, whether orally or in writing, that is designated as confidential or that reasonably should be understood to be confidential given the nature of the information and the circumstances of disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information shall mean only information explicitly designated as confidential by the Disclosing Party, regardless of its nature or the circumstances of disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the 'reasonably should be understood to be confidential' clause, significantly narrowing the scope of protection. Under German law, certain information is inherently confidential due to its nature. Removing this clause could lead to the unprotected disclosure of sensitive information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: § 17 of the German Act Against Unfair Competition (UWG)
perturbation.law_explanation: § 17 UWG protects trade secrets. The original definition allows for the protection of information that 'reasonably should be understood to be confidential', which aligns with trade secret protection principles. Removing that clause and requiring only explicit designation weakens the protection afforded by the contract, potentially conflicting with the legal obligation to protect trade secrets under German law. Some information is inherently confidential irrespective of labeling.

perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' by requiring explicit designation, which contradicts the principle that some information is inherently confidential, irrespective of labeling. The Law Explanation supports that the original definition aligns with trade secret protection principles and that removing the 'reasonably should be understood to be confidential' clause weakens the protection. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that § 17 UWG was repealed, but the Law Explanation still refers to it, indicating the general legal concept of trade secret protection and inherently confidential information remains relevant, thus the change in definition weakens the protection in the contract and create a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall use the Confidential Information solely for the purpose of evaluating and potentially entering into a business relationship with the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall use the Confidential Information solely for any reason it deems acceptable.
perturbation.explanation: The modification removes the limitation on use, allowing the Receiving Party to use the Confidential Information for any purpose. This directly contradicts the purpose for which the information was disclosed and undermines the entire agreement. It removes any restriction to only evaluating business relationships.
perturbation.contradicted_law: § 823 BGB (German Civil Code) - Tortious Interference with Business
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 823 of the BGB protects against unlawful interference with established business interests. Using confidential information received under a specific understanding (evaluating a business relationship) for any other reason, especially to the detriment of the disclosing party, could be considered an unlawful interference, particularly if it causes financial damage. The change allows for such interference, negating the protections the original agreement offers and potentially violating the BGB.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 823 BGB - Einzelnorm zurück weiter Nichtamtliches Inhaltsverzeichnis Bürgerliches Gesetzbuch (BGB) § 823 Schadensersatzpflicht (1) Wer vorsätzlich oder fahrlässig das Leben, den Körper, die Gesundheit, die Freiheit, das Eigentum oder ein sonstiges Recht eines anderen widerrechtlich verletzt, ist dem anderen zum Ersatz des daraus entstehenden Schadens verpflichtet. (2) Die gleiche Verpflichtung trifft denjenigen, welcher gegen ein den Schutz eines anderen bezweckendes Gesetz verstößt. Ist nach dem Inhalt des Gesetzes ein Verstoß gegen dieses auch ohne Verschulden möglich, so tritt die Ersatzpflicht nur im Falle des Verschuldens ein. zum Seitenanfang Impressum Datenschutz Barrierefreiheitserklärung Feedback-Formular

perturbation.justification: The modification allows the Receiving Party to use confidential information for 'any reason it deems acceptable,' which directly contradicts the original agreement that limits its use to evaluating a business relationship. This contradicts § 823 BGB, as it enables the Receiving Party to potentially cause financial damage or interfere with the Disclosing Party's rights ('sonstiges Recht eines anderen widerrechtlich verletzt,' as mentioned in Snippet 1 and Snippet 2). The altered text removes the protective measure the original contract provided, thus creating a pathway for unlawful actions against the Disclosing Party's business interests. Therefore, the modified clause fails to uphold the original legal intent and obligation.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall terminate one (1) year from the date hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall terminate when both parties subjectively feel like it should.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing a fixed term with a subjective feeling introduces unacceptable uncertainty into the contract. The termination is no longer based on an objective standard, making the agreement potentially unenforceable under German law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: § 314 BGB (German Civil Code) - Termination for Cause
perturbation.law_explanation: § 314 BGB discusses the termination of continuing obligations for cause. While this agreement isn't necessarily a 'continuing obligation' in the specific sense of § 314, the principle of needing objective and justifiable reasons for terminating a contractual relationship is important. Substituting 'subjectively feel like it should' for a fixed term introduces unacceptable arbitrariness. The term makes the termination clause too vague to be enforceable, creating conflict with the requirement of legal certainty in contractual relationships under German law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 314 BGB - Einzelnorm zurück weiter Nichtamtliches Inhaltsverzeichnis Bürgerliches Gesetzbuch (BGB) § 314 Kündigung von Dauerschuldverhältnissen aus wichtigem Grund (1) Dauerschuldverhältnisse kann jeder Vertragsteil aus wichtigem Grund ohne Einhaltung einer Kündigungsfrist kündigen. Ein wichtiger Grund liegt vor, wenn dem kündigenden Teil unter Berücksichtigung aller Umstände des Einzelfalls und unter Abwägung der beiderseitigen Interessen die Fortsetzung des Vertragsverhältnisses bis zur vereinbarten Beendigung oder bis zum Ablauf einer Kündigungsfrist nicht zugemutet werden kann. (2) Besteht der wichtige Grund in der Verletzung einer Pflicht aus dem Vertrag, ist die Kündigung erst nach erfolglosem Ablauf einer zur Abhilfe bestimmten Frist oder nach erfolgloser Abmahnung zulässig. Für die Entbehrlichkeit der Bestimmung einer Frist zur Abhilfe und für die Entbehrlichkeit einer Abmahnung findet § 323 Absatz 2 Nummer 1 und 2 entsprechende Anwendung. Die Bestimmung einer Frist zur Abhilfe und eine Abmahnung sind auch entbehrlich, wenn besondere Umstände vorliegen, die unter Abwägung der beiderseitigen Interessen die sofortige Kündigung rechtfertigen. (3) Der Berechtigte kann nur innerhalb einer angemessenen Frist kündigen, nachdem er vom Kündigungsgrund Kenntnis erlangt hat. (4) Die Berechtigung, Schadensersatz zu verlangen, wird durch die Kündigung nicht ausgeschlossen. Fußnote (+++ § 314: Zur Nichtanwendung vgl. § 10 Abs. 5 KredWG +++) zum Seitenanfang Impressum Datenschutz Barrierefreiheitserklärung Feedback-Formular

perturbation.justification: The change from a fixed term to subjective feelings for termination introduces unacceptable uncertainty, potentially making the agreement unenforceable under German law. The original text provides a clear end date, whereas the changed text allows termination whenever 'both parties subjectively feel like it should.' While § 314 BGB explicitly addresses termination of continuing obligations for cause, the principle it embodies—requiring objective and justifiable reasons for terminating a contractual relationship—is relevant here. The changed text undermines this principle by introducing unacceptable arbitrariness, contradicting the requirement of legal certainty in contractual relationships.
